title: Bidirectional Attention
description: An attention pattern that lets each token read context on both sides when the model is allowed to see the full sequence.
openingSummary: Bidirectional attention lets a token use context from both earlier and later positions, which makes it the standard attention pattern for encoder-style models that need to understand a whole sequence instead of predicting only the next token.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: Bidirectional attention is the full-context version of self-attention. When the mask allows it, each token can compare itself with tokens to its left and right instead of looking in only one direction.

whyItExists.title: Why It Exists
whyItExists.body: Bidirectional attention optimizes for representation quality when the model's job is to understand an entire sequence at once. It helps each position build a richer hidden state before any downstream prediction head reads it.

howItWorks.title: How It Works
howItWorks.body: Queries, keys, and values are computed as in ordinary self-attention, but the attention mask does not block later positions. In a masked language model or other encoder setting, the score matrix stays open across the whole visible sequence, so one token can gather evidence from earlier words, later words, and its own position.

mathOrComputeSchema.title: Math Or Compute Schema
mathOrComputeSchema.body: The formulas below contrast causal attention with bidirectional attention. The projection math stays the same, but the mask changes from next-token-only reach to a full-context mask that keeps both left and right context available.

comparedToNearbyModules.title: Compared To Nearby Modules
comparedToNearbyModules.body: Compared with causal attention, bidirectional attention removes the next-token-only restriction and uses full-sequence context. Compared with cross-attention, it still reads from the same sequence rather than a separate encoder memory. Compared with the broader attention page, this page focuses on the specific mask pattern that encoder-style models use.

exampleArchitectures.title: Example Architectures
exampleArchitectures.body: Bidirectional attention appears in encoder stacks and on the encoder side of encoder-decoder systems, where the model must build a contextual representation of the input before a later component consumes it. That is why it is the natural fit for reading-style tasks, masked language models, and many Transformer pipelines that separate understanding from generation.

limitationsAndTradeoffs.title: Limitations And Tradeoffs
limitationsAndTradeoffs.body: Because tokens can read future positions, plain bidirectional attention does not match next-token generation. Decoder-only language models therefore use causal masks instead when they must predict text one token at a time.

whyItStillMatters.title: Why It Still Matters
whyItStillMatters.body: Many readers first meet transformer attention through autoregressive chat models, so a dedicated bidirectional page prevents full-context encoder behavior from being hidden behind decoder-first explanations.

dimensions.sequenceSource: Where keys and values come from

bidirectional.visibleContext: Both earlier and later tokens in the same visible sequence
bidirectional.bestFitTask: Encoder-style understanding and masked language model objectives
bidirectional.sequenceSource: The same sequence as the query position

attention.visibleContext: Depends on the architecture and mask pattern chosen for the attention block
attention.bestFitTask: General attention lookup across many Transformer designs
attention.sequenceSource: Usually the same sequence for self-attention, but the overview spans broader patterns

mha.visibleContext: Dense attention over whatever positions the chosen mask leaves visible
mha.bestFitTask: Baseline per-head attention when the model keeps one key-value pair per query head
mha.sequenceSource: The same sequence as the query position in self-attention blocks

gi.definition: Bidirectional mask that keeps both left and right context available inside the visible sequence.
